section.title: The Canaanite Woman and the Four Thousand
section.sectionRationale: Right after teaching that defilement is of the heart, Jesus crosses into Gentile territory. A Canaanite woman's persistent, humble faith wins a healing for her daughter, and then Jesus feeds four thousand on a Gentile-leaning shore. The gospel is beginning to reach beyond Israel.
section.thesis: The bread of the children is not so scarce that the dogs cannot be fed too: a Gentile woman's great faith and a second feeding on the far shore reveal that the mercy of Israel's Messiah overflows to the nations.
section.frameNote: Two scenes: the faith of the Canaanite woman, whose daughter is healed; and the feeding of the four thousand, an abundance of bread for the crowds.

verses.text: Leaving that place, Jesus withdrew to the district of Tyre and Sidon.
And a Canaanite woman from that region came to Him, crying out, “Lord, Son of David, have mercy on me! My daughter is miserably possessed by a demon.”
But Jesus did not answer a word. So His disciples came and urged Him, “Send her away, for she keeps crying out after us.”
He answered, “I was sent only to the lost sheep of the house of Israel.”
The woman came and knelt before Him. “Lord, help me!” she said.
But Jesus replied, “It is not right to take the children’s bread and toss it to the {{kynarion|dogs}}.”
“Yes, Lord,” she said, “even the dogs eat the crumbs that fall from their master’s table.”
“O woman,” Jesus answered, “your {{pistis|faith}} is great! Let it be done for you as you desire.” And her daughter was healed from that very hour.

units.label: The feeding of the four thousand

verses.text: Moving on from there, Jesus went along the Sea of Galilee. Then He went up on a mountain and sat down.
Large crowds came to Him, bringing the lame, the blind, the crippled, the mute, and many others, and laid them at His feet, and He healed them.
The crowd was amazed when they saw the mute speaking, the crippled restored, the lame walking, and the blind seeing. And they glorified the God of Israel.

Then Jesus called His disciples to Him and said, “I have {{splanchnizomai|compassion}} for this crowd, because they have already been with Me three days and have nothing to eat. I do not want to send them away hungry, or they may faint along the way.”
The disciples replied, “Where in this desolate place could we find enough bread to feed such a large crowd?”
“How many loaves do you have?” Jesus asked. “Seven,” they replied, “and a few small fish.”
And He instructed the crowd to sit down on the ground.
Taking the seven loaves and the fish, He gave thanks and broke them. Then He gave them to the disciples, and the disciples gave them to the people.
They all ate and were satisfied, and the disciples picked up seven basketfuls of broken pieces that were left over.
A total of four thousand men were fed, besides women and children.
After Jesus had dismissed the crowds, He got into the boat and went to the region of Magadan.

kynarion.gloss: A little dog, a household pet rather than a street scavenger. Jesus' word is gentler than the usual term for dog, and the woman seizes on it: even the little dogs under the table eat the children's crumbs. It frames Israel's priority without slamming the door on the nations.

pistis.gloss: Faith, trust, reliance. Jesus commends the woman's faith as great, a rare praise in Matthew, given not to a Jewish leader but to a Gentile woman who clings to His mercy despite every obstacle.

splanchnizomai.gloss: To be moved with compassion from the depths, literally a stirring in the inward parts. It is Jesus' visceral mercy for the hungry crowd, the same compassion that drives His healing and feeding throughout the Gospel.

basic.context: Jesus leaves Jewish territory and enters the region of Tyre and Sidon. A Canaanite woman begs Him to heal her demon-tormented daughter. He seems to refuse, testing her, and she answers with humble, persistent faith: even the little dogs eat the crumbs. Jesus praises her great faith and heals her daughter. Then, on a mountain by the sea, He heals many and feeds a crowd of four thousand with seven loaves and a few fish, with baskets left over.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the woman's story as a test that draws out and displays her faith, not as a cold rejection. Notice the movement of the chapter: from clean and unclean, to a Gentile woman, to a feeding on Gentile-leaning ground. The theme is the mercy of Israel's Messiah beginning to overflow to the nations, even before the Great Commission.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus uses the softer word kynarion, little household dogs, not wild curs, and the woman turns it into an argument for grace. Jesus calls her pistis, her faith, great. At the feeding He is moved with splanchnizomai, gut-deep compassion, for the hungry crowd.
basic.typology: Jesus feeding the multitude in a desolate place echoes God feeding Israel with manna in the wilderness. The abundance, with baskets left over, shows Him as the true bread who satisfies, and the second feeding, reaching toward the Gentiles, foreshadows a table set for all nations.
basic.christ: Jesus is the Son of David whose mercy cannot be confined to Israel. He tests and honors faith, He heals the broken, and He feeds the hungry from His own hand. He is the bread of life, and the crumbs from His table are enough to save.
basic.god: God's plan runs to Israel first and then to the nations. He is not stingy: the children's bread is so abundant that the dogs are fed too. He delights in humble, persistent faith and answers it, and He is glorified as the God of Israel by a Gentile crowd.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 1:16 speaks of the gospel to the Jew first and also to the Greek. Matthew 8 shows another Gentile, the centurion, whose faith Jesus marvels at. Isaiah 25 and 56 promise a feast and a house of prayer for all nations. The manna of Exodus 16 stands behind the feeding.
basic.oneStory: Between His teaching on the heart and His journey to the cross, Jesus lets the mercy of the kingdom spill over Israel's borders. A Gentile woman's faith and a second feeding announce quietly what the risen Christ will command openly: this bread is for the nations, and whoever comes in faith will be filled.

context.literary: The scene is placed deliberately after the teaching that food and hands cannot defile: if the old purity barriers no longer separate, then a Canaanite can come to the table. Matthew pairs the healing with a second feeding, echoing the feeding of the five thousand in chapter 14 but on the far, Gentile-leaning shore, so that the two feedings frame Jesus' mercy to Jew and Gentile alike.
context.historical: Canaanite is Matthew's pointed old name for the ancient enemies of Israel; the woman is a Gentile from pagan territory. Jews sometimes called Gentiles dogs, but Jesus uses the diminutive for a household pet. The region of the four thousand lay toward the Decapolis, largely Gentile ground, and the crowd glorifies the God of Israel as outsiders would.
context.canonical: The passage embodies the pattern of Romans 1:16, to the Jew first and also to the Greek. It fulfills prophetic hopes of Gentiles streaming to Israel's God, Isaiah 56, and of a feast for all peoples, Isaiah 25, and it anticipates the Great Commission of Matthew 28 and the mission of Acts.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To display a Gentile's great faith and Jesus' overflowing mercy, showing that while salvation comes to Israel first, it is destined for the nations, and to reveal Jesus as the compassionate provider of true bread.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes Jesus testing and rewarding faith; it does not prescribe that God will always delay before answering. The abiding truths are the value of humble persistence in prayer and the reach of Christ's mercy beyond every barrier.

debates.question: Why did Jesus at first seem to refuse the Canaanite woman?

views.label: A genuine expression of His mission's order
views.case: Jesus was truly sent first to the lost sheep of the house of Israel; His words state the God-ordained sequence, Israel then the nations, which His silence and reply make plain.

views.label: A deliberate test to draw out her faith
views.case: Jesus' delay and hard saying function to elicit and display the woman's remarkable persistence and humility, ending in His public praise, so the whole exchange showcases faith rather than rejects the woman.

debates.bearing: Both hold together: Jesus honors the order of His mission and, within it, tests and then magnifies a faith that grasps His mercy, a preview of the nations coming in.

study.originalLanguages: The woman calls Jesus Lord, Son of David, Israel's own royal title, though she is a Canaanite. When Jesus speaks of tossing the children's bread to the kynarion, the little dogs of the house, she does not take offense but presses the image: even the little dogs eat the crumbs that fall. Jesus answers, your pistis, your faith, is great, a commendation He gives to almost no one else. At the feeding He is moved with splanchnizomai, a compassion felt in the depths, for a crowd that has nothing to eat, and from seven loaves He fills them with baskets to spare. The mercy of Israel's Messiah is proving wide enough for the world.

typology.fulfillment: As God fed Israel manna in the wilderness, Jesus feeds the crowd, revealing Himself as the true bread of life, Exodus 16, John 6.

typology.type: The crumbs from the master's table
typology.fulfillment: The overflow of covenant blessing to the Gentiles, a foretaste of the feast for all nations, Isaiah 25:6.
The nations, once enemies and far off, drawn in through faith in Christ, Ephesians 2:11-13.

study.christ: Jesus is the Son of David whose kingdom mercy overflows Israel's borders. He tests faith not to crush it but to bring it into the open and honor it, and He gives what is asked. He is moved to the depths by human need and meets it with His own hand, breaking bread until all are satisfied and baskets remain. In Him the true bread has come, and even the crumbs of His table are life; the Gentile who clings to Him in faith is not turned away but filled, and the God of Israel is glorified among the nations through the works of His Son.
study.god: The God of Scripture keeps the order of His promises, to Israel first, yet He was always aiming at the nations. He is generous beyond calculation: the children's bread is not diminished when the dogs are fed. He delights to answer humble, persistent faith, and He is not the private possession of one people but the God whom a Gentile crowd glorifies with amazement. His compassion, embodied in Jesus, reaches the tormented daughter, the lame and blind, and the hungry multitude alike.

crossReferences.note: The gospel is the power of God for salvation, to the Jew first and also to the Greek.
The centurion, another Gentile whose great faith Jesus marvels at and rewards.
Foreigners joined to the LORD, gathered to a house of prayer for all peoples.
Jesus as the bread of life, which the wilderness feedings foreshadow.

study.oneStory: The mercy of the kingdom, taught in the previous scene to be a matter of the cleansed heart, now spills visibly over Israel's borders. A Canaanite woman's faith is honored, and a second multitude is fed on Gentile ground, glorifying the God of Israel. What is quiet here becomes the risen Lord's open command in chapter 28: go to all nations. The one story moves from Israel's table to a feast for the world, and the bread is Christ Himself.

quiz.q: What was remarkable about the Canaanite woman?

options.text: As a Gentile she showed great, humble, persistent faith in Israel's Messiah.
options.note: Verses 22-28 highlight her faith, which Jesus calls great, despite every obstacle.

options.text: She was a Gentile who came to Jesus for help.
options.note: True and significant, but it omits the heart of the scene, the greatness of her faith.

options.text: She argued cleverly enough to change Jesus' mind against His will.
options.note: Her words are humble faith, not a debate that overpowers Jesus; He was drawing out her faith.

options.text: She demanded healing as something she deserved.
options.note: She claimed nothing as her due, only the crumbs of mercy, the opposite of demanding her rights.

quiz.q: What does Jesus' saying about the children's bread and the dogs reveal?

options.text: Salvation comes to Israel first, yet its abundance overflows to the Gentiles.
options.note: The image keeps Israel's priority while the woman rightly sees mercy spilling to the nations.

options.text: That Jesus cared only about the people of Israel.
options.note: He does state Israel's priority, but the scene ends with mercy reaching a Gentile, so this is too narrow.

options.text: That Gentiles were worthless in God's eyes.
options.note: Jesus uses the gentle word for a household pet and honors her faith; the point is order, not contempt.

options.text: That faith makes no difference to how Jesus responds.
options.note: Her faith is exactly what Jesus commends and rewards.

quiz.q: What moved Jesus to feed the four thousand?

options.text: Deep compassion for the hungry crowd who had been with Him three days.
options.note: Verse 32 says Jesus had compassion, unwilling to send them away faint.

options.text: A desire to prove His power with a miracle.
options.note: The feeding does display His power, but the stated motive is compassion, not display.

options.text: The disciples' request that He provide bread.
options.note: The disciples doubted it could be done; the initiative and compassion were Jesus'.

options.text: The crowd's demand that He feed them.
options.note: The crowd made no such demand; Jesus acted out of His own compassion.

quiz.q: What is the significance of the second feeding on the far shore?

options.text: Jesus' provision reaches toward the Gentiles, foreshadowing bread for all nations.
options.note: Placed after the Canaanite woman and toward Gentile territory, it signals the widening of mercy.

options.text: Jesus simply repeated an earlier miracle for a new crowd.
options.note: It is a real second feeding, but the placement and setting carry the added meaning of reaching the nations.

options.text: It shows the disciples had finally learned to trust Him fully.
options.note: The disciples again doubt there is enough bread, so this misreads their response.

options.text: It proves that Jesus fed only the people of Israel.
options.note: The setting leans toward Gentile ground, and the crowd glorifies the God of Israel as outsiders.

questions: The woman refused to give up when heaven seemed silent. Where do you need to keep bringing a request to Jesus even when the answer seems delayed?
She asked only for crumbs and received great mercy. How does approaching God in humility, rather than entitlement, change the way you pray?
Jesus called her faith great. What obstacle to faith, in your background, failures, or circumstances, is Jesus inviting you to press through to Him?
The same compassion that fed the crowd meets your needs. Where do you need to trust that Christ sees your hunger and will provide?
The gospel overflows every border. Who around you might feel like an outsider to God's mercy, and how could you carry the bread of Christ to them?

translationNotes.note: Canaanite is Matthew's deliberate archaic term for the woman's people, the ancient pagan inhabitants of the land, underscoring that she is a Gentile far outside the covenant.
The word rendered dogs is the diminutive for little household dogs or puppies, not wild scavengers; the gentler term is what the woman builds her humble reply upon.
The region of Magadan is otherwise unknown and appears as Magdala or Dalmanutha in some manuscripts and parallels; the exact location is uncertain but lay by the Sea of Galilee.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Matthew 15:21-39. Verse text assembled by concatenating segment values in order; heading, line-break, and note markers were skipped. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
